text: The surgeon visually inspects the pelvic organs, identifying two intramural fibroids, one pedunculated subserous fibroid, and one small anterior fibroid. Diluted vasopressin is injected using a pisat needle, with careful monitoring for blood aspiration and pulse oximeter readings.

The surgeon uses an umbilical optical port and a midline port for fibroids less than ten centimeters. An 11 mm myomascrew is inserted through the midline port to provide traction towards the umbilical valve, minimizing the risk of harm to vital structures in case of slippage.

The surgeon uses a monopolar device to incise the fibroid wall, carefully addressing small feeding vessels to prevent retraction into the myometrium. In the case of a subserous fibroid, the thin myometrial layer covering the fibroid is shelled out, while preserving the myometrium is prioritized for deep intramural fibroids. The fibroid is progressively detached, sacrificing a small amount of myometrium when necessary.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully separate the fibroid from the myometrium, applying traction with a myoma screw and occasionally switching to a sturdy tenaculum for larger fibroids. Feeding vessels and small fibers attaching the fibroid to the base are meticulously managed to ensure hemostasis before complete detachment.

The surgeon uses a monopolar electrocautery with pure cutting current set between forty-five to fifty to make an incision in the myometrium, achieving adequate depth for enucleation. The fibroid is grasped with a sharp tenaculum or myoma screw, applying traction until it is nearly detached from the base. Hemostasis is secured using small bursts of energy at low settings before removing the fibroid. The procedure is repeated for other fibroids, with incisions made to accommodate the widest diameter of the myoma, ensuring minimal damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the third sub-serous anterior fibroid, ensuring awareness of the bladder margins and the fibroid's position relative to the round ligament. Traction is applied using a sharp instrument, tenaculum, or myoma screw to create space between the uterus and fibroid, minimizing injury to the myometrium and bladder. Monopolar pure cutting current is used to detach the fibroid, with attention to controlling small oozes to prevent capillary retraction and excessive bleeding.

The surgeon addresses a bleeding defect by suturing it first, using an extracorporeal knot tying technique with a monofilament PDS suture. A 30 mm curved needle is inserted through an 11 mm valveless trocar, with two needle holders used for precise tissue manipulation. The surgeon employs a tooth grasper for secure tissue handling, and a knot pusher to complete the suturing process, ensuring the defect is closed efficiently.
